sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 5

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 5:1
items.html:
The thought of creation necessitates all the particulars of reality to emerge from one another, until the end of correction.
Now that we have been granted all the above, we can understand, if only faintly, the might of His unity — that "My thoughts are not your thoughts," etc. — and that all the multiplicity of matters and forms that we perceive in this whole reality before us is united in Him in one single thought, namely the thought of creation, "to delight His creatures." This single thought encompasses the whole of reality in complete unity until the end of correction, for it is the entire purpose of creation, as above. It is the operator — that is, in the manner of the force of the operator present in the operated — for what with Him is only a thought becomes, in the creatures, a binding and necessary law. And since He thought to delight us, this matter was necessarily activated in us: to be receivers of His good abundance. It is also the operation — that is, once this law of the desire to receive pleasures was imprinted in us, we define ourselves by the name "operation," for through this disparity of form we pass from being Creator to being creature, from being operator to being operation, as above. It is also the toil and the work: by the force of the operator in the operated, as above, the quantity of the craving to receive in us grows and intensifies by way of the cascading of the worlds, down to the discernment of a separated body in this world — a form opposite to the Life of Lives, whose definition contains no bestowal outside itself whatsoever — which brings death to the bodies, and every kind of torment and toil to the soul, as will yet be written. And it is the matter of the work of the Creator in Torah and Mitzvot: through the illumination of the line [Kav] in the restricted place, the holy names, the Torah, and the Mitzvot extend; and through labor in Torah and Mitzvot in order to bestow contentment upon one's Maker, little by little the vessels of reception in us are inverted into vessels of bestowal. And this is all the reward we hope for: so long as the vessels of reception are uncorrected, we cannot open our mouths wide to receive His abundance, for fear of the disparity of form, in the secret of "one who eats of that which is not his own fears to look upon his own face" — for which reason the first restriction took place, as above. But when we correct our vessels of reception to be in order to bestow, we thereby equalize the vessels with their Maker, and become fit to receive His abundance without end.
Thus you see that all the opposite forms in this creation before us — the form of operator and operated, the form of the corruptions and the corrections, the form of the work and its reward, and so on — are all included only in His single thought, in utmost simplicity: precisely "to delight His creatures," no less and no more. And in the same way there are also included in that thought all the multitude of conceptions — those of our holy Torah and those of the external wisdoms — and all the multitude of creatures and worlds and the differing conducts in each of them: all emerge and flow only from that single thought, as I shall explain in the course of our commentary, each in its place.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 5:2
items.html:
Malchut of Ein Sof means that Malchut does not make an end there.
By the above we can understand what is brought in the Tikkunei Zohar concerning Malchut of Ein Sof — at which the doorposts shook from the voice of the wonderers: how can the name Malchut be applied within Ein Sof? For if so, the nine upper Sefirot are there too, etc. By our words it is well clarified: the desire to receive necessarily included in the Light of Ein Sof, as above, is what is called Malchut of Ein Sof — but there, Malchut did not make an end and a boundary upon the Light of Ein Sof, since no disparity of form due to the desire to receive was yet revealed in her. Therefore it is called Ein Sof [no end], meaning that Malchut makes no end there — as distinct from what is after the restriction and below, where in every Sefira and every Partzuf an end is made by the force of Malchut.
